In more general situations, the wavefunction can be expressed as a
, with time evolution given by the phase factors of the eigenstates:

children.html: ψ(x,t)=∑ncnψn(x)e−iEnt/ℏ\psi(x,t) = \sum_n c_n \psi_n(x) e^{-i E_n t / \hbar}ψ(x,t)=∑n​cn​ψn​(x)e−iEn​t/ℏ

Time Dependence of a Pure Eigenfunction

children.label: Time Dependence of a Pure Eigenfunction

children.value: Until now, we have largely ignored the time dependence of quantum systems. This is because we have primarily focused on pure eigenfunction states,

children.html: ψn(x,t)\psi_n(x,t)ψn​(x,t)

children.value: , which exhibit no time-dependent behavior for observable quantities. The reason for this is straightforward.

children.html: ψn(x,t)=ψn(x)e−iℏEnt\psi_n(x,t) = \psi_n(x) e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t}ψn​(x,t)=ψn​(x)e−ℏi​En​t

children.value: In any probabilistic calculation, the time dependence vanishes because the expressions involve the square of the wavefunction. The absolute value of the complex time-dependent phase factor equals unity:

children.html: ∣ψn(x,t)∣2=ψn∗(x)ψn(x)e−iℏEnte+iℏEnt=∣ψn(x)∣2\mid \psi_n(x,t) \mid^2 = \psi_n^*(x)\psi_n(x) e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} e^{+\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} = \mid \psi_n(x) \mid^2∣ψn​(x,t)∣2=ψn∗​(x)ψn​(x)e−ℏi​En​te+ℏi​En​t=∣ψn​(x)∣2

children.value: Similarly, for the expectation value of an operator

children.html: A^\hat{A}A^

children.value: , the time-dependent phases cancel out, leaving only the spatial part:
\langle A \rangle = \int \psi_n^*(x) e^{+\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} \hat{A} \psi_n(x) e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} dx = \int \psi_n^*(x) \hat{A} \psi_n(x) dx
children.html: ⟨A⟩=∫ψn∗(x)e+iℏEntA^ψn(x)e−iℏEntdx=∫ψn∗(x)A^ψn(x)dx\langle A \rangle = \int \psi_n^*(x) e^{+\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} \hat{A} \psi_n(x) e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} dx = \int \psi_n^*(x) \hat{A} \psi_n(x) dx⟨A⟩=∫ψn∗​(x)e+ℏi​En​tA^ψn​(x)e−ℏi​En​tdx=∫ψn∗​(x)A^ψn​(x)dx

children.value: Will we always have this fortunate cancellation of time dependence? The answer is no. When the system is described as a
of eigenstates, time dependence reappears in a significant way.
Time Dependence of a Superposition of Eigenfunctions

children.label: Time Dependence of a Superposition of Eigenfunctions

children.value: Let us start with a superposition state of two energy eigenfunctions at time

children.html: t=0t=0t=0
∣ψ(0)⟩=c1∣1⟩+c2∣2⟩\mid \psi(0) \rangle =c_1\mid 1 \rangle + c_2 \mid 2 \rangle∣ψ(0)⟩=c1​∣1⟩+c2​∣2⟩

children.value: What would the state be after some time

children.html: ttt

children.value: ? By virtue of separation of variables, we add the time dependence as a multiplicative factor for each eigenfunction:
\mid \psi(t) \rangle =c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\mid 1 \rangle + c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\mid 2 \rangle= c_1(t)\mid 1\rangle+c_2(t) \mid 2 \rangle
children.html: ∣ψ(t)⟩=c1e−iℏE1t∣1⟩+c2e−iℏE2t∣2⟩=c1(t)∣1⟩+c2(t)∣2⟩\mid \psi(t) \rangle =c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\mid 1 \rangle + c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\mid 2 \rangle= c_1(t)\mid 1\rangle+c_2(t) \mid 2 \rangle∣ψ(t)⟩=c1​e−ℏi​E1​t∣1⟩+c2​e−ℏi​E2​t∣2⟩=c1​(t)∣1⟩+c2​(t)∣2⟩

children.value: Thus we see that the coefficients change over time. This means our state vector moves in the functional space spanned by the fixed orthogonal eigenfunctions! We can verify that this expression satisfies the time-dependent Schrödinger equation by plugging it in.
Proof that the superposition obeys the time-dependent Schrödinger equation

children.html: iℏ∂∂t∣ψ⟩=H^ψ(t)⟩i\hbar\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\mid \psi \rangle =\hat{H}\psi(t)\rangleiℏ∂t∂​∣ψ⟩=H^ψ(t)⟩

children.value: i\hbar \Big( -\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\mid 1\rangle-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\mid 2\rangle \Big) = E_1 c_1(t)\mid 1 \rangle +E_2 c_2(t)\mid 2 \rangle
children.html: iℏ(−iℏE1c1e−iℏE1t∣1⟩−iℏE2c2e−iℏE2t∣2⟩)=E1c1(t)∣1⟩+E2c2(t)∣2⟩i\hbar \Big( -\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\mid 1\rangle-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\mid 2\rangle \Big) = E_1 c_1(t)\mid 1 \rangle +E_2 c_2(t)\mid 2 \rangleiℏ(−ℏi​E1​c1​e−ℏi​E1​t∣1⟩−ℏi​E2​c2​e−ℏi​E2​t∣2⟩)=E1​c1​(t)∣1⟩+E2​c2​(t)∣2⟩

children.value: c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\hat{H}\mid 1 \rangle + c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\hat{H} \mid 2 \rangle = E_1 c_1(t)\mid 1 \rangle +E_2 c_2(t)\mid 2 \rangle
children.html: c1e−iℏE1tH^∣1⟩+c2e−iℏE2tH^∣2⟩=E1c1(t)∣1⟩+E2c2(t)∣2⟩c_1 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_1 t}\hat{H}\mid 1 \rangle + c_2 e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_2 t}\hat{H} \mid 2 \rangle = E_1 c_1(t)\mid 1 \rangle +E_2 c_2(t)\mid 2 \ranglec1​e−ℏi​E1​tH^∣1⟩+c2​e−ℏi​E2​tH^∣2⟩=E1​c1​(t)∣1⟩+E2​c2​(t)∣2⟩

children.value: Certain quantities remain invariant under time evolution. For instance, it is natural to expect that normalization does not change over time: the particle must always be located somewhere within the box at any given moment.

children.html: ∣ψ(t)⟩=∑ncne−iℏEnt∣n⟩\mid \psi(t)\rangle = \sum_n c_n e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} \mid n\rangle∣ψ(t)⟩=n∑​cn​e−ℏi​En​t∣n⟩

children.value: The normalization condition is expressed as:

\langle \psi(t) \mid \psi(t)\rangle = \sum_n \sum_k \langle n \mid c^*_n e^{\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} \cdot c_k e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_k t} \mid k\rangle = \sum_n \sum_k c^*_n c_k e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}(E_k - E_n)t} \delta_{kn} = \sum_n \mid c_n \mid^2 = 1
children.html: ⟨ψ(t)∣ψ(t)⟩=∑n∑k⟨n∣cn∗eiℏEnt⋅cke−iℏEkt∣k⟩=∑n∑kcn∗cke−iℏ(Ek−En)tδkn=∑n∣cn∣2=1\langle \psi(t) \mid \psi(t)\rangle = \sum_n \sum_k \langle n \mid c^*_n e^{\frac{i}{\hbar}E_n t} \cdot c_k e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}E_k t} \mid k\rangle = \sum_n \sum_k c^*_n c_k e^{-\frac{i}{\hbar}(E_k - E_n)t} \delta_{kn} = \sum_n \mid c_n \mid^2 = 1⟨ψ(t)∣ψ(t)⟩=n∑​k∑​⟨n∣cn∗​eℏi​En​t⋅ck​e−ℏi​Ek​t∣k⟩=n∑​k∑​cn∗​ck​e−ℏi​(Ek​−En​)tδkn​=n∑​∣cn​∣2=1

children.value: In the final step, we use the fact that the eigenfunctions are orthogonal, meaning

children.html: ⟨n∣k⟩=0\langle n \mid k \rangle = 0⟨n∣k⟩=0
n≠kn \neq kn=k

children.value: , so the cross terms vanish and only the diagonal terms, where

children.html: n=kn = kn=k

children.value: Quantities that remain conserved over time are called
. In quantum mechanics, we typically deal with expectations or averages rather than sharply defined values.
However, the principle of energy conservation dictates that the
must be a conserved quantity. To determine whether a particular quantity depends on time, we take the time derivative of the expectation value:

children.html: ⟨A⟩=⟨ψ∣A^∣ψ⟩\langle A \rangle = \langle \psi \mid \hat{A} \mid \psi \rangle⟨A⟩=⟨ψ∣A^∣ψ⟩

children.value: The time derivative of this expectation value is:

children.html: ∂∂t⟨A⟩=⟨∂ψ∂t∣A^∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣A^∣∂ψ∂t⟩+⟨ψ∣∂A^∂t∣ψ⟩\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\langle A \rangle = \langle \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} \mid \hat{A} \mid \psi \rangle + \langle \psi \mid \hat{A} \mid \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} \rangle + \langle \psi \mid \frac{\partial \hat{A}}{\partial t} \mid \psi \rangle∂t∂​⟨A⟩=⟨∂t∂ψ​∣A^∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣A^∣∂t∂ψ​⟩+⟨ψ∣∂t∂A^​∣ψ⟩

children.value: Now, using the time-dependent Schrödinger equation,

children.html: iℏ∂∂t∣ψ⟩=H^∣ψ⟩i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \mid \psi \rangle = \hat{H} \mid \psi \rangleiℏ∂t∂​∣ψ⟩=H^∣ψ⟩

children.value: we can express the time derivatives of the bras and kets as:

children.html: ⟨∂ψ∂t∣=−1iℏ⟨ψ∣H^,\langle \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} \mid = -\frac{1}{i\hbar} \langle \psi \mid \hat{H},⟨∂t∂ψ​∣=−iℏ1​⟨ψ∣H^,
∣∂ψ∂t⟩=1iℏH^∣ψ⟩.\mid \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} \rangle = \frac{1}{i\hbar} \hat{H} \mid \psi \rangle.∣∂t∂ψ​⟩=iℏ1​H^∣ψ⟩.

children.value: Substituting these into the time derivative of the expectation value, we obtain:

children.html: ∂∂t⟨A⟩=1iℏ⟨ψ∣A^H^∣ψ⟩−1iℏ⟨ψ∣H^A^∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣∂A^∂t∣ψ⟩\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\langle A \rangle = \frac{1}{i\hbar} \langle \psi \mid \hat{A}\hat{H} \mid \psi \rangle - \frac{1}{i\hbar} \langle \psi \mid \hat{H}\hat{A} \mid \psi \rangle + \langle \psi \mid \frac{\partial \hat{A}}{\partial t} \mid \psi \rangle∂t∂​⟨A⟩=iℏ1​⟨ψ∣A^H^∣ψ⟩−iℏ1​⟨ψ∣H^A^∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣∂t∂A^​∣ψ⟩

∂∂t⟨A⟩=1iℏ⟨ψ∣[A^,H^]∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣∂A^∂t∣ψ⟩.\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\langle A \rangle = \frac{1}{i\hbar} \langle \psi \mid [\hat{A}, \hat{H}] \mid \psi \rangle + \langle \psi \mid \frac{\partial \hat{A}}{\partial t} \mid \psi \rangle.∂t∂​⟨A⟩=iℏ1​⟨ψ∣[A^,H^]∣ψ⟩+⟨ψ∣∂t∂A^​∣ψ⟩.

children.html: A^\hat{A}A^

children.html: ∂A^∂t=0\frac{\partial \hat{A}}{\partial t} = 0∂t∂A^​=0
[A^,H^]=0[\hat{A}, \hat{H}] = 0[A^,H^]=0
